compiled_state.title: As a developer, write_commit_event runs bash-native with preserved flock semantics and byte-exact JSON event output
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
## Description

**What**: Replace `write_commit_event`'s three Python processes (JSON generation, flock acquisition, git commit) with bash-native equivalents: `flock(1)` with `LOCK_EX -w 30` and a 2-retry loop for locking; escape-safe `printf`-based JSON generation byte-for-byte compatible with `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False, separators=(',',':'), sort_keys=True)`; direct `git commit` invocation. Make an explicit decision on the ticket-lib.sh / ticket-lib-api.sh duality: either host the new `write_commit_event` in `ticket-lib.sh` in place (so the 9 existing sourcing scripts pick it up unchanged), or convert `ticket-lib.sh` into a thin shim that sources `ticket-lib-api.sh` and re-exports the function. Preserve the function's public signature, exit codes, and side effects exactly.

**Why**: `write_commit_event` runs on every write (create/link/transition/comment/tag/untag/edit) and is the dominant cost for write ops — the three Python processes sum to 240–450ms per call. Bash-native rehoming unlocks the SC1 write target. The single-library decision is required to satisfy SC3 (no logic duplication) and to avoid silently breaking the 9 scripts that currently source `ticket-lib.sh` (including `emit-review-event.sh` and `suggestion-record.sh`, which are not ticket subcommands).

**Scope**:
- IN: Bash-native `flock(1)` with LOCK_EX semantics, 30s timeout × 2 retries (matches existing Python fcntl.flock contract); bash-native JSON generation for the event body that is byte-for-byte identical to `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False, separators=(',',':'), sort_keys=True)` for any ticket content including shell-special chars (quotes, backslashes, newlines, unicode), verified via randomized-input testing over ≥50 inputs with md5 comparison; direct `git commit` invocation; explicit decision that `ticket-lib.sh` either contains the new function in place or shims to `ticket-lib-api.sh`; the 9 existing consumers (`ticket-create.sh`, `ticket-edit.sh`, `ticket-tag.sh`, `ticket-transition.sh`, `ticket-untag.sh`, `ticket-comment.sh`, `ticket-link.sh`, `emit-review-event.sh`, `suggestion-record.sh`) continue to source `ticket-lib.sh` unchanged; preserved function signature and return codes; `DSO_TICKET_LEGACY=1` routes through the pre-refactor Python flock+commit path.
- OUT: Library expansion to other write ops (Story D); read ops (Story D); platform matrix (Story F); extension pattern documentation (Story D).

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, `write_commit_event` spawns zero `python3` processes on the default path — verified by subprocess-count test coverage extending `tests/scripts/test-ticket-subprocess-count.sh`
  ← Satisfies: "Write-path operations ... complete in <0.05s per call"
- When this story is complete, the flock contract per `plugins/dso/docs/contracts/ticket-flock-contract.md` is preserved verbatim (LOCK_EX, 30s timeout × 2 retries, single global per-worktree lock at `.tickets-tracker/.ticket-write.lock`) and the existing concurrency test suite passes unmodified
  ← Satisfies: "Flock contract preserved verbatim ... concurrent-write safety validated by the existing concurrency test suite"
- When this story is complete, ≥50 randomized ticket inputs (including quotes, backslashes, newlines, unicode) produce event JSON whose md5 matches the md5 of `json.dump(ensure_ascii=False, separators=(',',':'), sort_keys=True)` output for the same input
  ← Satisfies: "Tickets containing shell-special characters ... round-trip correctly through all write and read operations"
- When this story is complete, `emit-review-event.sh` and `suggestion-record.sh` continue to work end-to-end — verified by integration tests that exercise both scripts against the new `write_commit_event`
  ← Satisfies: "All existing ticket-CLI test assertions ... pass unmodified"
- When this story is complete, writing a ticket event via the new path and immediately reading the ticket via `ticket show`/`ticket list` returns the new event (no stale `.cache.json` reducer state) — verified by a read-after-write consistency test
  ← Satisfies: "All existing ticket-CLI test assertions ... pass unmodified"
- When this story is complete, setting `DSO_TICKET_LEGACY=1` routes `write_commit_event` through the pre-refactor Python flock+commit path — verified by a test that toggles the flag and asserts Python code paths execute
  ← Satisfies: "A `DSO_TICKET_LEGACY=1` env flag reverts to the pre-refactor Python-backed path"
- When this story is complete, a ticket CLI write op completes in <0.05s per call on Linux reference hardware measured via hyperfine over 10 warm runs (vs the A0 baseline)
  ← Satisfies: "Write-path operations ... complete in <0.05s per call"
- When this story is complete, unit tests are written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations

- [Reliability] ticket-flock-contract.md MUST remain the authoritative specification; Story H updates the doc to describe the bash-native implementation explicitly
- [Reliability] Reducer cache `.cache.json` invalidation must survive the bash-native write path — read-after-write consistency test is the guard
- [Consumer Impact] `emit-review-event.sh` + `suggestion-record.sh` are not ticket subcommands — integration tests must exercise them, not just the ticket CLI surface
- [Performance] SC1 <0.05s target applies to Linux reference hardware; git-commit intrinsic floor may exceed 50ms on macOS or Alpine — per-platform baselines in Story G handle this

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
